build_from.reason: ExecutorConfig (lines 206-225) and validateExecutor (lines 593-677) already define the single-executor schema and its validation. Multi-tier support is a schema/validation extension here, not a new subsystem.
build_from.suggested_approach: Extend Registry.Executor (currently a single *ExecutorConfig pointer, config.go:463-466) into either an ordered slice of tiers or add ceiling fields (max_estimated_minutes, max_severity_for_fix as an upper bound alongside the existing min_severity_for_fix floor) directly on ExecutorConfig. Wire the routing decision into generateFixes (internal/verify/executor.go:104-232), which already performs a per-finding eligibility filter (confidence + severity floor) that a complexity ceiling check slots into naturally. Skipped findings must stay visible via logPipelineWarning (internal/verify/pipeline.go:41) — a new warning class alongside executor_unknown_provider / executor_fix_failed — rather than being silently dropped.

existing_patterns.name: Effective-value resolver methods
existing_patterns.description: ExecutorConfig fields with a nil/empty zero value resolve to a documented default via a dedicated method: EffectiveMaxToolCalls, EffectiveFixMinSeverity, EffectiveExecutorTimeoutSecs, EffectiveExecutorTemperature (internal/registry/config.go:231-279).
existing_patterns.follow_for: Any new ceiling field (e.g. max_estimated_minutes) should get its own EffectiveXxx() resolver method rather than inline nil-checks at call sites, matching the existing convention.

existing_patterns.name: Per-finding eligibility filter before dispatch
existing_patterns.description: generateFixes loops findings and applies a chain of skip checks (confidence >= HIGH, severity >= floor, not already fix-attributed) before dispatching work to a bounded worker pool (internal/verify/executor.go:128-149).
existing_patterns.follow_for: A complexity-ceiling check (est_minutes <= max_estimated_minutes) is a natural fourth skip condition in this same chain — skipped findings should be logged the same way, not silently dropped.

existing_patterns.name: Config validation with named byte/length/range caps
existing_patterns.description: validateExecutor enforces explicit numeric ranges and length caps (MaxExecutorPersonaLen, MaxTimeoutSecs, MaxExecutorSystemPromptLen, MaxExecutorRules, MaxExecutorRuleLen, MaxExecutorToolCalls) as named constants near the top of config.go (internal/registry/config.go:593-677).
existing_patterns.follow_for: New ceiling fields need the same named-constant + explicit range-check treatment for consistency with existing validation style.

existing_patterns.name: Pipeline-warning + FixWarning skip visibility
existing_patterns.description: Non-fatal executor-stage skips/failures are surfaced two ways: logPipelineWarning (internal/verify/pipeline.go:41) emits a classed Warn through the context logger — classes in use include executor_unknown_provider, executor_agent_mode_fallback, executor_fix_failed, executor_truncated_fix, executor_empty_fix (internal/verify/executor.go:114,177,190,200,206) — and JSONFinding.FixWarning (internal/reconcile/emit.go:136, json:"fix_warning,omitempty") carries a per-finding warning into findings.json and report output.
existing_patterns.follow_for: A ceiling skip must add a new logPipelineWarning class (e.g. executor_ceiling_skip) with the finding's File:Line in the detail, so over-ceiling findings remain visible in logs/output instead of reading as a false clean run — plan Risk 3 requires this explicitly.

related_files.purpose: Defines ExecutorConfig (lines 206-225), Registry.Executor single-pointer field (463-466), effective-value resolvers (231-279), and validateExecutor (593-677).
generateFixes (lines 104-232) is the sole fix-execution routing point: filters findings by confidence/severity/attribution (lines 128-149), then dispatches to the executor.

logPipelineWarning (line 41) is the single classed-warning sink generateFixes already uses for executor-stage skips/failures; a ceiling-skip warning class is invoked from executor.go but follows this helper's contract (class at Warn, path-bearing detail at Debug).
meetsSeverityFloor (lines 14-22) does the existing severity-rank comparison generateFixes uses; a complexity ceiling check is a sibling helper, not a replacement.
Finding.EstMinutes int (line 53) is already parsed from the reviewer's model-emitted stream (7-column schema, ModelColumns=7 at line 112; parsed at line 235 via atoiOrZero).

JSONFinding.EstMinutes int with json:"est_minutes" tag (line 69) already carries the estimate through to findings.json output; populated at lines 213, 266, 359. JSONFinding.FixWarning (line 136) is the per-finding warning channel skip visibility can reuse.
Merge logic takes MAX(EstMinutes) across duplicate findings from multiple reviewers (line 65, reclib.MaxEstMinutes).

Base reviewer prompt template. Lines 37-45 already instruct every persona to emit EST_MINUTES as column 6 of a 7-column pipe-delimited schema ('an integer estimate to fix'). This is the file the original epic's T1 incorrectly targeted (community_schema.go, which does not exist).
Documents the per-source and reconciled column layouts including EST_MINUTES semantics (best-effort int, non-numeric parses as 0, max wins on merge) at lines 23, 39, 62.
'Executor (fix generation, active in 7.0)' section (lines 341-392) documents the current single-executor config surface as a field table; assumes exactly one executor for the whole run.

Existing test coverage for ExecutorConfig parsing/defaults/validation (TestExecutor_InvalidMinSeverityForFix line 112, TestExecutor_MinSeverityForFixExplicitAndNormalized line 61, TestExecutor_MaxToolCallsOutOfRangeRejected line 543) — the pattern new ceiling-field tests should follow; the MaxToolCalls range test is the closest precedent for a numeric ceiling field.

Existing generateFixes test coverage (e.g. TestGenerateFixes_SkipsBelowSeverity line 145, TestGenerateFixes_PopulatesFixAndAttribution line 72) — the pattern a new TestGenerateFixes_SkipsAboveComplexityCeiling should follow.
Example registry config demonstrating the current single-executor block; a multi-tier example config should be added alongside or in place of this.

integration_points.description: The existing skip-condition chain (confidence, severity floor, fix-attribution) is where a complexity-ceiling skip condition and tier-fallback routing must be added. Note the chain currently skips silently (bare continue) — the ceiling skip must log through logPipelineWarning (internal/verify/pipeline.go:41) so skipped findings stay visible.
New ceiling fields (max_estimated_minutes, and any multi-tier list structure) need validation wired in here, following the existing named-constant range-check pattern.
Currently a single *ExecutorConfig. This is the field that must become either a slice/ordered-list of tiers or gain sibling ceiling fields for a fallback chain.

integration_gaps.description: min_severity_for_fix floors which findings are attempted, but nothing caps the upper end: a cheap/local executor is dispatched against every finding that clears the floor regardless of EstMinutes. Add max_estimated_minutes (validated like the existing fields, with its own EffectiveXxx() resolver) so over-ceiling findings are skipped before dispatch.

The existing skip chain (confidence, severity floor, fix-attribution at internal/verify/executor.go:128-149) drops findings with a bare continue — no logPipelineWarning call, no FixWarning. A complexity ceiling added to this chain would inherit that silence; plan Risk 3 (a false 'clean' run when every tier's ceiling is set too low) requires an explicit skip log, and test coverage should assert the warning is emitted.

integration_gaps.gap: No two-tier executor example or routing documentation
integration_gaps.description: examples/registry-with-executor.yaml demonstrates a single executor only, and docs/registry.md's Executor section (lines 341-392) documents no ceiling field or routing semantics. AC4's cheap-tier-then-frontier-tier workflow needs a worked example (two independently-configured runs against the same findings.json) plus doc updates to docs/registry.md and docs/findings-format.md.

reusable_components.name: EstMinutes field (stream + reconcile)
reusable_components.usage_example: AC1 of the original epic ('Reviewer agents output an estimated complexity/time metric for every finding') is already satisfied by this existing field — reviewer personas emit it today via personas/_base.md's 7-column schema. No new schema/prompt work is needed for AC1; the plan should route on the existing JSONFinding.EstMinutes value (internal/reconcile/emit.go:69) rather than inventing a new complexity_score field.

reusable_components.name: meetsSeverityFloor
reusable_components.usage_example: Rank-based comparison helper (lines 14-22); a complexity-ceiling check (EstMinutes <= ceiling) is a simpler integer comparison that can sit alongside it in the same filter chain.

reusable_components.name: logPipelineWarning
reusable_components.usage_example: Classed pipeline warnings (helper defined at line 41) (executor_unknown_provider, executor_fix_failed, executor_truncated_fix, executor_empty_fix) share one slog sink with path-at-debug discipline. A ceiling skip adds a new class string at the call site — no new logging plumbing needed.

architecture_notes: AC1 from the original epic plan is already satisfied by existing code: reviewer personas emit EST_MINUTES today (personas/_base.md:37-45), it's parsed (internal/stream/parser.go:235) and carried through to JSONFinding.EstMinutes (internal/reconcile/emit.go:69). The plan's real net-new scope is AC2-AC4: config surface for complexity ceilings/multi-tier chaining, the routing engine change in generateFixes, and skip-logging.

Registry.Executor is a single pointer field today (internal/registry/config.go:463-466) with an explicit doc comment distinguishing nil (no fix generation) from configured. Multi-tier support requires a real schema decision: convert to a slice of named tiers (breaking change, needs migration/back-compat consideration) vs. keep single executor + add a max_estimated_minutes ceiling that causes a skip-and-log rather than true multi-executor chaining (additive, no breaking change, smaller surface). This is a design decision that should be surfaced as a clarification rather than assumed.

generateFixes (internal/verify/executor.go:104-232) already runs findings through a bounded worker pool keyed off reg.Verify.MaxParallel; a routing/ceiling check is a filter-time decision, not a concurrency-model change.

The original epic's cited file internal/personas/community_schema.go does not exist (confirmed by /refine-epic on 2026-07-17); the real schema/prompt files are internal/stream/parser.go, internal/reconcile/emit.go (structs) and personas/_base.md (prompt contract) — already fully wired for EstMinutes. (Note: internal/personas/community_schema_test.go does exist, but it exercises ValidateCommunityPersonaYAML, which lives in internal/registry/validate.go:49 — the absence of community_schema.go is real, not a naming miss.)
generateFixes lives only in internal/verify/executor.go — the original epic's reference to internal/fanout/engine.go for this function was incorrect (also confirmed by /refine-epic).

If the design clarification chooses true in-process multi-executor chaining over the additive ceiling, the codebase already has an ordered-fallback precedent: the fanout engine's Primary → Fallbacks agent chain (internal/fanout/engine.go, Epic 19.5). That precedent serves reviewer agents, not the executor — borrowing its shape for executor tiers is a design decision, not a requirement; the ceiling approach needs no fanout changes.

Skip visibility has an established mechanism, not a greenfield one: logPipelineWarning (internal/verify/pipeline.go:41) for the log line plus JSONFinding.FixWarning (internal/reconcile/emit.go:136) when the skip should travel with the finding into findings.json/report output. The plan's skip-logging requirement (T4/AC3) should reuse these rather than introduce a new reporting channel.

files_to_modify.reason: Add complexity-ceiling field(s) to ExecutorConfig (or convert Registry.Executor to a tiered structure per the design decision above) and extend validateExecutor accordingly.

Extend generateFixes's per-finding filter chain to evaluate EstMinutes against the executor's ceiling and skip findings that exceed it — logging each skip via logPipelineWarning (new class, e.g. executor_ceiling_skip); if multi-tier chaining is chosen, route skipped findings to the next tier.
Add a sibling helper (e.g. meetsComplexityCeiling) alongside meetsSeverityFloor for the new check, keeping filter logic testable in isolation.
Document the new ceiling field(s)/tier structure in the existing Executor config reference table (lines 341-392).
Cross-reference EstMinutes' new consumer (executor routing) if the semantics of the field's use expand beyond TD-table display.
Add a documented example of the new ceiling/tier configuration.

test_patterns.example_test: internal/verify/executor_test.go:145 (TestGenerateFixes_SkipsBelowSeverity) — direct precedent for a new TestGenerateFixes_SkipsAboveComplexityCeiling; internal/registry/executor_config_test.go:543 (TestExecutor_MaxToolCallsOutOfRangeRejected) — direct precedent for the ceiling field's range-validation test
